primary_diagnosis.name: Deep vein thrombosis of the right lower limb (provoked, long-haul-flight associated), pending duplex ultrasound confirmation

pathophysiology_summary: Prolonged immobility on a 14-hour long-haul flight produces venous stasis which, together with the hypercoagulability and endothelial factors of Virchow's triad, promotes thrombus formation in the deep veins of the calf. The occluding thrombus impairs venous return, producing the unilateral swelling, deep aching pain, warmth and erythema of the affected limb. The two dangers are (1) proximal propagation and embolisation of thrombus to the pulmonary arteries causing life-threatening pulmonary embolism, and (2) rarely, massive iliofemoral thrombosis (phlegmasia) threatening limb viability. Diagnosis rests on structured pre-test probability (Wells score) plus confirmatory compression duplex ultrasonography (a non-compressible deep vein), and treatment is prompt anticoagulation to prevent propagation and embolisation.

differential.diagnosis: Deep vein thrombosis of the lower limb
differential.evidence_basis: Acute unilateral calf swelling with deep aching pain, warmth and erythema is the classic DVT presentation.
Also causes a red, warm, swollen leg — the principal mimic to distinguish; classically has a demarcated advancing edge and a skin breach/portal of entry, and higher fever.
Can mimic DVT with sudden calf swelling and pain, but usually there is a history of knee arthritis/effusion and no major immobility risk factor.
Considered for calf pain, but a strain usually has a sudden onset during exertion rather than the gradual onset here, and would not explain the marked swelling and warmth.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening, DVT should already lead given a unilateral swollen painful calf; the AI Doctor must simultaneously hold cellulitis as the key mimic and screen for pulmonary embolism.

differential.diagnosis: Deep vein thrombosis of the lower limb (provoked)
differential.evidence_basis: Recent 14-hour flight (a strong provoking immobility factor) plus unilateral swelling, tenderness and warmth gives a high pre-test probability (high Wells score).
Remains on the list, but the absence of a skin breach/portal of entry and only a low-grade temperature make it less likely than DVT here; the two can also coexist.
No prior knee joint disease and a strong immobility risk factor make this unlikely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Eliciting the long-haul-flight immobility is the single highest-value history item — it converts a swollen calf into a high-probability provoked DVT.

differential.diagnosis: Deep vein thrombosis of the lower limb (provoked), no current pulmonary embolism features
differential.evidence_basis: PE screen negative (no chest pain, breathlessness or haemoptysis, SpO2 99%), so the presentation is an isolated lower-limb DVT — but PE remains the important-not-to-miss complication to safety-net.
The lethal complication of DVT. Currently no features, but any new breathlessness, pleuritic chest pain, haemoptysis or syncope must trigger immediate emergency escalation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A negative PE screen sets the correct baseline tier at an urgent same-day in-person assessment (not a 000 ambulance) while making the PE safety-net mandatory.

differential.diagnosis: Deep vein thrombosis of the right lower limb (provoked, flight-associated), pending duplex ultrasound
differential.evidence_basis: High Wells pre-test probability; confirmatory compression duplex ultrasonography (non-compressible deep vein) and anticoagulation are required — neither is achievable via telehealth, so urgent in-person assessment is mandatory.
Retained as a differential/co-existing possibility for the in-person clinician; must not be treated with antibiotics in place of investigating and anticoagulating the suspected DVT.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The failure mode is anchoring on cellulitis (and prescribing antibiotics) or under-triaging as a muscle strain, either of which leaves a DVT untreated with interval PE risk.

red_flags.description: Features of pulmonary embolism — sudden breathlessness, pleuritic chest pain, haemoptysis, syncope or a fast heart rate
red_flags.clinical_significance: PE is the lethal complication of DVT; it is the single most important thing to actively screen for and to safety-net, because it can develop after the consultation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An untreated propagating DVT can embolise to the lungs causing collapse and sudden death.

red_flags.description: Limb-threatening massive thrombosis (phlegmasia) — a severely swollen, tense limb that becomes cold, pale, dusky/blue or numb
red_flags.clinical_significance: Massive iliofemoral thrombosis can compromise arterial inflow and threaten limb viability — a vascular surgical emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed recognition can lead to venous gangrene and limb loss.

red_flags.description: Acute unilateral calf swelling and pain with a strong provoking immobility factor — high pre-test probability of deep-vein thrombosis
red_flags.clinical_significance: A high Wells probability mandates same-day confirmatory imaging and anticoagulation; it cannot be safely deferred to a routine appointment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Deferring assessment allows thrombus propagation and embolisation in the interval.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on the red, warm, swollen leg as cellulitis and prescribing antibiotics, missing the DVT — the classic mimic trap; the absence of a skin breach and only a low-grade temperature argue against cellulitis.
Dismissing the calf pain as a pulled muscle and advising rest/routine review, leaving a high-probability DVT untreated with interval PE risk.

Attempting to confirm or exclude DVT remotely (e.g. via a phone 'Homan's sign' or reassurance) instead of arranging the duplex ultrasound and in-person assessment that the diagnosis actually requires.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: False reassurance from a negative PE screen
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating the absence of chest/breathing symptoms as making the leg safe to defer — PE can develop later, so the negative screen lowers the tier but never removes the mandatory safety-net.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: High probability (Wells >=2): entire-leg swelling, calf-circumference discrepancy >3 cm, localised tenderness along the deep venous system, recent immobilisation, with no more-likely alternative diagnosis.
risk_scores.clinical_implication: High pre-test probability warrants proceeding directly to compression duplex ultrasound and, per local pathways, considering interim anticoagulation if imaging is delayed.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Objective calf-circumference discrepancy (measured 4.5 cm; right 41 cm vs left 36.5 cm in the source)
Palpable tender venous cord along the deep posterior tibial vein
Distension of superficial collateral veins on examination
Extent of pitting oedema to the knee
Blood pressure and respiratory rate without a device
Compression duplex ultrasound (non-compressible deep vein — the confirmatory test)
Baseline bloods before anticoagulation (FBC, renal function, coagulation profile)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a high-probability provoked DVT and arrange an URGENT same-day in-person assessment (urgent care / GP with same-day access, or ED if no same-day imaging pathway is available) for Wells scoring, compression duplex ultrasound and initiation of anticoagulation. Give a mandatory pulmonary-embolism safety-net: call 000 immediately for any breathlessness, pleuritic chest pain, coughing up blood or feeling faint. Advise leg elevation and to avoid massaging/rubbing the calf. The AI Doctor must NOT itself prescribe anticoagulation or attempt to exclude DVT remotely.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for suspecting DVT (the constellation is classic); the real risks are anchoring on the cellulitis mimic, under-triaging to a routine appointment, or over-triaging a stable DVT without PE features to a 000 ambulance.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Wells PS et al. — clinical model for predicting DVT pre-test probability
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Stratify pre-test probability; high probability proceeds to compression ultrasound and consideration of interim anticoagulation.
Confirm DVT with compression ultrasonography and treat with a direct oral anticoagulant unless contraindicated; safety-net for pulmonary embolism.
